INTRODUCTION TO YOUTUBE
When Google purchased YouTube for $1.65 billion in 2006, many in the business world
thought it was a little crazy. After all, the video sharing service had been around for less
than two years at the time.
Google was betting on one simple fact: online video would become the most consumed
form of content in the world. At this point it is safe to say that they bet right. Billions of
people use YouTube every month, with over 1 billion hours of video being consumed on a
daily basis.
4
At this point, upwards of 90% of companies are using online video as a part of their
marketing. That said, just because companies are creating videos, doesn’t mean that they
are capitalizing on them. In this manual you’ll not only learn how to make effective videos,
but how to leverage YouTube to generate more fans, more leads, and more sales.
Today, Grant Cardone is an absolute force on YouTube. While his success certainly did not
come overnight, our team has analyzed his victories as well as his challenges from over
5000 videos. Within this manual, as well as in the supporting documents and videos, you
will get everything you need to maximize your YouTube results in minimal time.
Of all the social media platforms, I believe YouTube is far
and away the most valuable. When someone watches your
videos, they have a REAL connection with your brand.
Grant’s numbers for the most important YouTube statistics
Regardless of your company or cause, YouTube is the best place for you to share your
message. The rewards for growing an audience are massive, if you’re willing to do what it
takes. If you’re ready to start the journey, let’s get to work!

GETTING STARTED ON YOUTUBE
11
Your first step in your YouTube journey is to set up your Channel. Your channel is your
account on YouTube and includes all of your videos, your profile, and other ways for
your views to interact with your content.
If you don’t yet have a channel, creating one is free and easy. As you may know,
YouTube is owned by Google. Here are the steps to creating your channel:
Your Google account is ultimately a Google email address. You can either use a Google
account you already have, or if you are starting fresh, you will want a new email
address. If your company uses Google for email, you can ask the administrator to
create a new email. If you don’t have Google email, you can create one for free
(10xresource.com/yt4).
SETTING UP YOUR CHANNEL
CREATING A NEW CHANNEL
STEP 1
Create a Google account

CHANNEL CUSTOMIZATION
STEP 2
Create your channel and
choose a name
To create a new channel simply go to YouTube.com while you are logged into your
Google account. Click on the profile picture in the top right and then click Create a
channel.
Once you create your channel it will ask for a channel name and a profile picture.
Don’t get held up on these steps as you can always edit them later. It is worth noting
that you shouldn’t edit your channel name too often, as YouTube limits name edits to
3 every ninety days.
If you have a channel that has videos and significant subscribers and views, then
continue using it. If you have a channel that does not have a lot of activity, it is
recommended that you create a new channel. It is hard to get momentum on an
inactive channel. You can always download the videos from your old channel and add
them to your new one.
Your YouTube channel includes a profile page that serves as the main hub for all your
content. Users are able to access your channel profile by clicking on your profile
picture or account name.
All of the following channel components can be edited from within the Customization
section of YouTube Studio. YouTube Studio is where you will manage your entire channel
as well as all your content. To access YouTube Studio, click the profile picture in the top
right, then click the Customization icon on the left (you may need to scroll down).
Here is a screenshot of Grant’s channel profile. We’ve provided a brief description of the
primary components along with tips on how to optimize the setup of each.
Frequently Asked Question:
I created a channel in the past, should I create a new one?

CHANNEL SETTINGS
CHANNEL SETTINGS TAB
When you first create your channel, you will be assigned a default channel web
address (such as youtube.com/s8edje38d). Once your channel has been live for thirty
days and reached 100 subscribers you are eligible for a custom URL (such as
youtube.com/grantcardone). This is done from the Basic info tab inside customize.
In addition to customizing your channel, it is highly recommended that you review
and edit any desired settings. There are many settings in YouTube Studio. You should
review them all, but we will only discuss the most important ones here. To access
settings click the gear icon on the left side.
KEYWORDS
Add 10 to 15 phrases that you believe your ideal audience would search for on YouTube.
Use a variety of one word, two word, and three word phrases. We will discuss keyword
strategy more in the next chapter.
ADVANCED SETTING
While no action is needed here, there are preferences you may want to edit. For
example, if you can choose to hide your subscriber count until you’ve made more
progress.
FEATURE ELIGIBILITY
There are certain features, most importantly the ability to add video thumbnails and live
stream, that require you to verify your phone number. Do this right away.

The very first thing you need to get clear on is exactly who your audience is. On
YouTube, the viewer is everything. If you don’t know who you’re talking to, you won’t
create the right content and you won’t get your desired result.
While many of the so-called gurus on YouTube will tell you that you’ve got to find the
perfect niche, the truth is that there isn’t one. There are billions of people on YouTube,
so your audience is out there. While some audiences are certainly larger than others,
the bigger the audience, the bigger the competition.
AUDIENCE AND RESEARCH

Generally speaking, we believe you can have success serving any audience as long as…
Remember, it’s not about the type of content you want to make, it’s about the viewer.
It may seem like a subtle change of perspective, but it makes all the difference in your
success. In fact, we highly recommend that you develop a few imaginary characters, what
are referred to as personas, to help you gain a crystal clear picture of who is watching.
Here is a quick illustration to help make it real…
Needless to say, this is very specific, and for good reason. At this point, there are plenty
of people teaching woodworking (or virtually anything) on YouTube. You don’t need to
appeal to everyone, you just need to identify your avatar and give him or her exactly
what they want.
I make woodworking how-to videos
I make how-to videos for people who are new to woodworking
I make videos for “Carl the Crafter” who recently got turned on to
woodworking, but only has very basic tools. Carl works out of his garage
and primarily on the weekends because he works as a financial planner
during the week. While he does like to give his creations as gifts to family
members, he primarily does it because he finds it therapeutic. Since he is
older and new to the craft, he gets very frustrated by other woodworking
videos that go too quickly and skip the details.
You have passion and/or knowledge for the topic
(or you’re willing to learn).
You are clear on the value you provide your audience
(entertain, educate, etc).
Providing that value aligns with your personal goals
(i.e. you can monetize).
BAD
BETTER
BEST

While we’ll spend a lot of time discussing tactics and tips, if you want to be successful on
YouTube there is one thing you need to realize…
This is true for a few reasons:
MINDSET AND STRATEGY
The #1 secret to success on YouTube is consistency.
YouTube favors channels that produce content on a regular basis.
Creating consistent videos keeps your audience engaged and growing.
It requires lots of practice to create a habit and get good at YouTube.

If you worry about people not liking you on YouTube, you’re
never going to make it. I adopted the mindset of loving my
haters, because they fuel me more than my supporters.
The reality is that anybody can be successful on YouTube. That said, it isn’t the skills
and the knowledge that hold people back. Those things come with time. In order to
become successful on YouTube, you’ve got to develop the mindset that you are in it for
the long haul.
If you are coming into this with concern about how you are going to look, or what
people will say in comments, you are not alone. Every successful YouTuber starts in the
exact same place. Here are some tips to turn your concerns into a winning mindset...
It took years for Grant to crack the code and get his first 100 million views.
SHIFT YOUR PERSPECTIVE
Fear of Being a Failure ―→ Enjoy Process of Growth
Doing it for Personal Gain ―→ Doing it for Your Audience

Your intention is everything. When I started on YouTube it
wasn’t to make more money immediately, it was to build a
following. I sacrificed a lot of short-term cash, but it worked.
CREATE YOUR STRATEGY
Up to this point, we’ve intentionally focused more on your audience than on you.
While it is absolutely possible to become a viral star overnight, success on YouTube
rarely comes quickly. It is important to explore why you’re doing this in the first place.
QUESTION 1: What are your short-term goals?
Examples: Increase sales of my product, increase status in industry, help people succeed.
QUESTION 2: What are your long-term goals?
Examples: Become #1 in my industry, write a best-selling book, pass on my wisdom.
QUESTION 3: How committed are you to success?
Examples: Create 2 new videos weekly, learn all needed skills, never quit improving.

CONTENT TYPES
In addition to being the world’s largest video sharing platform, YouTube is also a social
network that offers a variety of ways to share and interact. In this section we will
discuss YouTube’s various features and functions.
STANDARD VIDEOS
The majority of content you post on YouTube will likely be regular videos as they will
get the most views long term. You can upload any video that is less than 12 hours and
256 GB, as long as it meets community guidelines (10xresource.com/yt5) and you verify
your account (10xresource.com/yt6).
VIDEO “SHORTS”
This feature was likely created in response to the popularity of TikTok. Shorts are videos
that are less than 60 seconds and are played vertically instead of horizontally. The
reason for this is that they are primarily recorded and viewed with mobile devices.
When a user views a short, they can quickly scroll through a large number of other
shorts by scrolling down on their phone.
UNDERSTANDING YOUTUBE
23
CONTENT AND ENGAGEMENT
FACT: The vast majority of YouTube are now viewed on mobile.

© 2021 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
When Shorts first came out we jumped on it and it blew my
channel up. The biggest lesson was to always capitalize on
new features, because YouTube will favor them.
Important Note: When adding a Short video, make sure to include #shorts in the
title or description to increase your chances of it being recommended by YouTube.
What makes Shorts exciting is the fact that they are more likely to be discovered than
standard videos. Keep in mind though, while Shorts can give you a quick boost in views
and subscribes, their popularity is often short-lived. Also, users are usually less engaged
than with a standard video since they are scrolling.
YOUTUBE LIVE
Live streaming on YouTube is a great way to build an active audience. You can reply to
comments and interact with viewers in real time. This feature can be used for a wide
variety of things, including streaming events, live shows, sales webinars, auctions, and
anything else you can imagine.
VIDEO “SHORTS”
This feature was likely created in response to the popularity of TikTok. Shorts are videos
that are less than 60 seconds and are played vertically instead of horizontally. The reason
for this is that they are primarily recorded and viewed with mobile devices. When a user
views a short, they can quickly scroll through a large number of other shorts by scrolling
down on their phone.
At 10X headquarters, we will often use YouTube Live in conjunction with other live
platforms, such as Facebook and Instagram. You can do this using third-party streaming
tools like BeLive or Restream.

YouTube is the second most used search in the world, so there can be huge rewards
for understanding how to rank. While we promise not to get technical, it is important
to understand the basics of how recommendations work on YouTube. Let’s start with
some basic definitions:
So in other words, in order to get good at SEO, you’ve got to understand the basics of
what the algorithm is looking for. That said, the role of the algorithm goes far beyond
search. The algorithm decides which videos are displayed at any time, for any
particular user.
ALGORITHM AND SEO
Search Engine Optimization (SEO) - The process of maximizing
the number of views your videos gets by improving the rank of
your video in YouTube searches.
Algorithm - The automated equation and rules that YouTube
uses to determine which videos appear in search results and are
recommended to each individual user.

Of all the 5 elements, the camera is the one that people worry about the most.
Ironically, it is the thing that you probably need to worry about the least out of the
gates. The reality is that, thanks to advances in technology, nearly any smartphone can
get the job done.
The important thing with your iPhone is that you adjust the
camera settings. This will include lighting, quality, frame speed
and video quality. Every phone is different, and if you search
YouTube for phrases like “iPhone 13 video setting” you’ll learn
what you need.
While you may want to record at 4K, keep in mind that the higher the quality, the
more difficult it is to transfer. You’ll likely want to use Google Drive or Dropbox to
upload to the web and share files. Other than that, you’ll just want to order a quality
tripod so that you can be stabilized.

When it comes to professional cameras, the most important factor is always the lens.
While it will vary depending on the type of shot you want to get, you’ll generally want to
get either a wide lens or a tight lens. You’ll also need a backup battery, several memory
cards and a decent tripod.
Sound is probably the most overlooked element for new creators. As a rule, a video with
great sound and mediocre video will ALWAYS come out better than a crisp video with
lousy sound. There are 3 factors with sound...
Believe it or not, there is an art to properly placing a
lavalier mic to reduce noise and look nice.
Google “lavalier broadcast loop” for pointers!
Generally speaking, any Nikon or Cannon brand DSLR camera will
work great. Bottom line is that you’re going to get what you pay
for. A quick YouTube search on the topic of DSLR cameras will
give you a lot of insight, but be careful. Researching, selecting,
and learning new equipment can be very time consuming.
Advanced Camera - DSLR Camera
2 	Sound
A. MICROPHONE QUALITY
Internal vs. External - It is almost always worth investing in a quality external
microphone, regardless of what type of camera you use. While new model
iPhones and smartphones have decent mics, you can add an external mic if
you’re using a professional video app (such as the one mentioned above).
Lavalier (Lapel) vs. Shotgun vs. Boom - The type of mic you use will vary
depending on what type of videos you create. In most cases, we recommend
using a good lavalier mic that attaches to the speaker’s shirt. If that doesn’t work,
the difference between a shotgun and a boom mic is where it is placed (shotgun
mics attach to the camera and boom mics are on a boom!)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 34 of 63 --

35
© 2021 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
Even if you plan to do-it-yourself, it can be a good idea to
hire a professional to help with initial setup. Once you’ve
got it dialed in, you can do it the same every time.
Omnidirectional vs. Unidirectional - Omnidirectional mics capture sounds from
all sides, while unidirectional takes the majority of sound from one way. While
unidirectional can be higher quality, they do run the risk of missing sound.
Generally speaking, lavaliers will be omnidirectional.
Dynamic vs. Condenser vs. Ribbon - This refers to how the mic converts sound.
You likely won’t need to worry about this unless you are using a boom mic.
Assuming you aren’t doing a major motion picture, you’ll likely use a boom if
you’re recording at a desk.
Wired vs. Wireless - When it comes to lavalier mics, there is obviously more
freedom and flexibility with a wireless version. That said, keep in mind that going
wireless creates potential issues, such as disconnection and lag in audio.
While it should go without saying, you do need to be sure to have as little
background sound as you can. Some of this can be removed in post-production,
make every effort you can to avoid wind, echo and other ambient noise.
B. BACKGROUND NOISE
Depending on what type of camera and mic you are using, there may be some
sound adjustments required. You’ll likely get instructions for this in the
instruction manual of your product, or by searching online. Above all else, always
test the audio before recording!
C. LEVELS AND SETTINGS
If you must record with wind, cover your mic with a furry
microphone known as a “dead cat”. If echo is an issue,
use blankets and foam on walls and floors.
If you’re making tutorial type videos, you’ll likely want to
use a condenser mic. A quality mic that is relatively
inexpensive is the Yeti by Blue.

BACKGROUND OPTIONS
Recording in front of a green screen lets you add any
background in post-production. While this can allow for
some cool effects, it comes out cheezy if done poorly.
Ultimately, if you’re making stationary videos, you can think of your background as
your studio. While you can shoot your videos anywhere, (and Grant always does!) it
is a good idea to have a “goto” setup where you can record consistently at any time.
These are usually solid colors such as white or gray, and can be purchased off of
Amazon for less than $20. This is the simple way to start and can look very professional,
especially if you are standing for your videos.
This can be done in your home by placing simple objects in the background. If you have
enough space, you can transform any room into a nice set with a little creativity. Keep in
mind that it is good to have a little space between the subject (the person in the video)
and the background.
Remember, you don’t have to have a major Hollywood setup on day 1!
When setting up your studio, you’ve got 2 basic options…

LIGHTING SETUP OPTIONS
No matter how good your camera is, if you don’t have correct lighting, then you won’t
look professional. Above all else, you want to make sure that your subject is always well
lit. There is nothing worse than having “racoon eyes” or awkward shadows distracting
from your content!
The thing about lighting is it is as much of an art as it is a science. While we’ll give you
tips, it will take practice.
Lighting is something that you can spend tremendous time and
money on, but for now, we’ll assume you are just getting started.
Here are two options…
If you are using your smartphone, and want to keep it ridiculously
simple, try using a 12” to 14” ring light. Make sure it has
“temperature” control. You can buy entire setups that even include
tripods for around $40 on Amazon.
Natural light can look the best, but it is the hardest to control.
For your studio, have a controlled and consistent light setup.
Lighting is all about the angles, keep trying until you get it.
Sometimes it is best to bounce light off of walls or ceilings.
If you are new, it is generally simpler to use LED lights.
Cover your lights with “soft boxes” to avoid harsh shadows.
Most decent lights will allow you to adjust white and yellow.
Avoid glare and shine on faces or objects whenever possible.

If you are doing a high production video, you may consider
using multiple cameras and angles. While it does bring a

The final aspect of filming is the subject. In most situations, this will be the featured

subject is human.

This will involve buying a few more lights. The advantage is
more control, especially on your background. While there are

configuration:
#2 More Advanced Setup
Camera Angle - While most beginners will position the camera straight in front of the
subject, it can be played with. For interview style videos, address the camera from roughly
a 45 degree angle. For important messages to the audience, especially a call-to-action,
always address the camera directly. Regarding positioning the camera vertically, if you
shoot from slightly below the speaker it helps demonstrate authority.
Subject Framing - In addition to the angle, the position of the subject in relation to the
viewing screen is also important. While the tendency is to place the subject in the middle,
most experts abide by the “law of thirds”. This common artistic and design principle states
that subjects placed at thirds of the screen, both vertically and horizontally, will demand
the most attention.
5 	Subject

MOVEMENT VS. STABILIZATION
Speaker Posture - The viewer gets a very different impression depending on whether
the speaker is sitting or standing. Sitting is very informal, and is great for storytelling and
building a connection. Standing is more action oriented and authoritative, so anytime
you are selling, we suggest you stand!
You’ll see that Grant often uses movements in his videos. This is great because he brings
the audience with him, which keeps them engaged. While this is great for impromptu
social media videos, and is an especially great way to start a video, in most studio
situations you’ll always want to have a tripod to maximize stability.
The very first decision you need to make is how often you are going to create videos. The
key is consistency, both for the algorithm and for your audience. Get committed to a
routine day and time you will post videos and publicise it to keep yourself on it!
Once you’re on a consistent production schedule, you’ll need to have a plan to ensure you
give yourself enough time for recording and editing. Think of it as an ongoing rotation of
production. To do this, you’ll want to have your video topics planned out weeks in
advance. Here are some tips to help...
Grant’s legendary “5 steps to becoming a millionaire” video
starts with him walking down the hall with the viewer.
SCHEDULE AND TOPICS
BEGINNER: Start with 1 new video per week.
ADVANCED: Create 2 to 3 videos per week.
PROFESSIONAL: Add a regular live stream show!

RECORDING PREPARATION
As you grow your channel and create more and more videos, you’re going to need to
stay extremely organized. While a notebook will work for a while, you’ll need to
progress either to an online spreadsheet (we use Google Sheets) or a task
management tool. It can also be helpful to use a calendar for scheduling both
recording and releasing videos.
Once you have your topic in mind, you’ll want to get prepared before your scheduled
video shoot. While your exact preparation will vary based on your personal style and
video type, you’ll always want your thoughts on paper in advance...
While we’ll cover the fine details of delivering an awesome performance in the next
chapter, here’s a super simple outline for creating your video bullet points...
CTA is short for call-to-action, which is the instructions you give the viewer to take your
desired action. You might also want to sprinkle them throughout the content
segment.
A Note on Organization
Want to be word-for-word? You need a teleprompter!
Don’t want to be scripted? Write bullet point outlines!
Hook the Use
Engagement CTA

The first 10-15 seconds are by far the most important part of
a video. It will determine if the video gets watched. Know
exactly how you plan to hook the viewer out of the gates.
WRITING GREAT HOOKS
Here’s a great formula for writing effective hooks…
a Callout Viewer + b Expected Outcome + c Open Loop
Callout Viewer - Talk to the ideal viewer and/or their specific situation.
Expected Outcome - Give a clear understanding of the benefit of watching.
Open Loop - Leave a bit of mystery that the user feels/needs to watch for.

ADVANCED VIDEO PLANNING
While many new YouTubers keep their videos simple, you may decide to create videos
with multiple storylines or layers. This is achieved by using multiple layers of
We’ll talk more about layering, including audio and music, in the video editing
chapter. For now, it is good to have a plan of what you need before going into the
shoot. This will help you be efficient and avoid having to reshoot things.
Canva offers a free storyboarding tool that helps you layout your story visually. This

A-roll footage - the primary video and audio of a video
B-roll footage - the secondary video that overlays a-roll footage
B-roll footage can either be original footage that you recorded, or

PERFORMANCE TIPS
While your bullet-pointed outline or script can be relatively simple, there are a number
of elements that you want to consider including. Not every video needs to include
everything listed below, but you should always take them into consideration.
If your video stays consistent throughout, your viewer will start to tune out. Interrupt
the normal flow by either inserting something unexpected, or by dramatically
changing your volume. You can add pattern interrupts in editing, but you’ll want to
plan ahead in case you need to record any b-roll footage.
Insert Pattern Interrupts
No matter your subject matter, people expect to be entertained on YouTube these
days. Chances are good they can see something similar elsewhere, so whoever brings
the most energy wins. Get creative. Even a bad joke is a good joke. Consider using
props or even outfits if it brings some life.
Humor and Personality
The beauty of video is that, unlike written word or audio, it allows you to connect
multiple senses. The old saying goes that “facts tell and stories sell” and that is
absolutely true. Get the user to see it, hear it, feel it, and even taste it. The more
sensory modalities you touch, the deeper your connection.
Stories and Use of Senses
There is a subtle art to establishing yourself as an expert, without bragging about it.
The goal is to provide context of your greatness. “While I’ve had some tremendous
successes, they’ve only come after extraordinary failures…” Even better is to highlight
stories of those who have used your product or service with success.
Credibility and Social Proof
While it is good to establish yourself, don’t think that your goal is to try to look flawless.
In fact, the more you learn to humble yourself, the more people will like you. Highlight
your challenges, without looking weak, and try to help the viewer actually feel what it
is like. People need to know that you’re human.
Vulnerability and Emotion

Don’t worry about being perfect. I found that the less I worry
about getting it just right, the more people relate. Your style
may differ, but I’m real and raw, and so are my videos.
This is something that takes a little practice. Rather than repetitively saying things like
“leave a comment” you can say, “I’m curious how you feel about this...” Remember to
keep it conversational and avoid sounding like a sales pitch. The reason you ask for
anything, including engagement, is for the viewer to value.
Remember that creating valuable content is only half the battle. Your goal is to create
a real connection with your viewer so they keep coming back for more!
Don’t Forget the CTAs!
TOP 10 SPEAKING TIPS
Rehearse your video outloud in advance to prepare
Start with something weird or unusual to grab attention
Imagine that you’re talking to one individual person
Hang your bullet points as close the camera as possible
Always frame your talking points clearly and resummarize
Be animated with your non-verbals (facial, hands, etc)
Vary your tonality, pacing and volume (not monotone)
Don’t feel a need to rush it, you can do multiple takes
Give your viewers a collective name to address them by
Create a cool sign off phrase that you say at the end

Adding b-roll, light music and graphics to a video can make
a massive difference in looking professional, but more

One of the more advanced features to learn on YouTube is the ability to add “end screens”
to your video. End screens are what you see at the very end of a video on YouTube. They
give you options such as viewing more videos or subscribing. Here is an example of one of
Grant’s end screens…
CREATING ENDSCREENS
Optionally “punch” in and out on subject to help vary the frame and
cover up cuts.
Optionally add “jump cuts” if you want to remove unwanted stutters
and pauses.
ROUND 2 - Frame and Refine
After video is completely edited into a linear storyline, optionally add
background music.
Lastly, you can optionally add graphics such as titles or “lower third”
(speaker names).
ROUND 3 - Music and Graphics

© 2021 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
It is important to understand that end screens work by overlaying various clickable
components OVER your video. For this reason, you’ll always want to add 20 extra seconds
to the end of your video to avoid the items overlapping your actual video.
You can either make the last 20 seconds have a design to fit with one of the layouts (see
below) OR you can keep it simple by just making the last 20 seconds a solid color like
black or white.
As you see below, there are a number of ways that your video can be found on YouTube.
At the end of the day though, the algorithm works similarly for all traffic sources in
deciding what to display.

VIDEO TITLES
There are two primary factors that determine which videos appear on YouTube. Videos
that follow this simple formula will appear in search results, as recommendations, in
browse and in feeds...
In order for your videos to get displayed, they must have both. A relevant video that does
not perform will virtually never be seen. A high performing video that does not relate will
get seen, but not nearly as much as it should.
All of the topics covered in this chapter will either help you improve the relevancy or
performance of your videos (or both).
Whenever possible, you should try to include your primary keyword phrase in your title.
That said, by far the most important aspect of a great title is that it increases the
click-through-rate (CTR).
Relevancy + Performance = Traffic
Relevancy Factors
� Title Keywords
� Description Keywords
� Tag Keywords
� Subtitle Keywords
� Video File Keywords
� Channel Keywords
Performance Factor
� Click Through Rate
� Average View Duration
� Total Watch Time
� Subscribes from Watch
� Time Stayed on YouTube
� Traffic Sent to YouTube

As you grow your channel and create more and more videos, you’re going to need to
stay extremely organized. While a notebook will work for a while, you’ll need to
progress either to an online spreadsheet (we use Google Sheets) or a task
management tool. It can also be helpful to use a calendar for scheduling both
recording and releasing videos.
It is worth getting professional thumbnails made as they can make or break your
video. If you want to save money, then consider using the online editor Canva
(10xresource.com/canva).

Bright colors and contrast, red pops the best

Arrows or some sort of action are always good

description, but always make sure every link is valuable.
Utilize the default upload feature in Settings to save

Subtitles are optional and it is a step that requires a little bit of effort. That said,
subtitles provide a lot of value and help your rank. The good news is that this process

When your transcription order comes back, you will want to download the version
that is labeled as SRT. This special file type includes the video timestamp in the text,
allowing YouTube to add subtitles to your video during the upload process. While this
sounds complicated, it actually is pretty simple.
ADDING SUBTITLES
Before you start uploading your video, you should go to Rev’s automated

If you are new to YouTube, you need to find any method you can to get your videos found.
While the following strategy does not always work, and should certainly not be depended

As you know, the largest traffic source on YouTube is recommended videos. Since YouTube
recommends videos based on similarity, the Piggyback Video Method increases your
chances of being recommended by “piggybacking” one particular video. This is done by
optimizing your video as follows...
In order to find a piggyback video, you’ll start by searching for a search term you want to
target. For our example, we will use “woodworking for beginners”.

© 2021 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
Don’t get bogged down by things that don’t move the
needle. If you don’t have clicks, then don’t spend too much
time with fancy tracking. Add stuff like this as you grow.
What is UTM? UTM is a long-time standardized method of capturing data for the
purposes of marketing attribution. In other words, it allows you to better know where
people came from when they become a subscriber or buy a product.
How does UTM work? UTM works by adding up to 5 data fields on the end of any URL.
This does not affect the functionality of the link in any way, but can be captured by your
CRM or email marketing system. Here are the 5 fields with examples of what each can
be used for…
How do I create the UTM links? While any link service such as Bitly or Rebrandly will
include UTM builders, you can also create links for free using Google’s own tool
(10xresource.com/yt13). Here is an example of a link with all 5 fields that will track back
to exactly where it came from…
How do I capture this data? While every system handles UTM data a little differently,
they will always provide instructions on how to do it. For example, here is the
documentation for the CRM that we use called Hubspot (10xresource.com/yt12).
Note that there is no exact way that you have to use the fields. The most important
thing is that you have a plan and you follow it. For the data to be valuable, it needs to
be consistent.
https://grantcardone.com/10x?utm_source=10xmarketing&utm_medium=guide&utm_campaign=
examplelink&utm_term=UTM&utm_content=tipstricksandhacks
1. 	Source* - Highest level, for example Google, Facebook, Instagram, etc.
2. Medium * - Next level, so for example ads, organic, etc.
3. Campaign* - References specific initiative, so could be product or promotion
4. Term - Used primarily for pay-per-click advertising for keyword terms
5. 	Content - Use primarily in social media or content to reference specific content
*Designates the required fields, others are optional
